text.title: We LOVE it here in PHNOM PENH, CAMBODIA! 🇰🇭 [Best Things We Did for Cheap]
text.u: after months of detouring through
Malaysia we found ourselves in a
completely different world one that
seems to go unnoticed in the shadow of
neighboring countries like Thailand and
Vietnam a place still reing from a
turbulent
past yet still full of the brightest
Smiles this is Cambodia and as cliche as
it sounds trust us when we say it's
nothing like you'd expect over the next
4 weeks we're taking you on a journey
through this country that is still
considered under ated and feels quite
special to us and we're starting right
in the capital of PanAm pen but before
we show you anything you have to be
introduced to this energetic
[Music]
city thank
you Cambodia streets are kind of a
beautiful Madness a thousand motorbikes
moving as one a dance with no
choreography no rules just Instinct a
agreement between Riders Tuk Tuk drivers
and pedestrians who step fearlessly into
the current so we just arrived in
Cambodia we hopped in a Tuk tuck right
out of the airport cost us $10 honestly
we have our bags and it's super early in
the morning so this was like an easy no
hassle option for us and it's always
amazing to see what they can carry on
these
motorbikes but after a short maybe
20-minute ride we arrived at our place
unpacked our clothes and went back out
to explore PanAm pin welcome to Cambodia
as you you can see it's pretty hectic
it's only 8:30 and there's so much
traffic out here but really exciting to
be in a new place so we're going to do a
lot of exploring today and try some new
foods and see what else we can
[Music]
find first stop this morning is the old
market now this is more of a local
market and not necessarily A touristy
place but it's a good way to get a
glimpse of what life around here is like
in the morning they sell a lot of fresh
produce so maybe we can find some
breakfast and some coffee along the way
funny thing about travel you see things
you wouldn't think you'd ever eat having
now experienced a small section of the
world these past few months I'm willing
to try almost anything
[Music]
[Applause]
once Breakfast in America and Southeast
Asia couldn't be more different not just
the ingredients but the entire
philosophy behind the first meal of the
day in the US breakfast is often a sugar
balm or protein heavy Diner style Feast
think stacks of syrup drench pancakes
sugary cereals and the classic bacon
eggs and toast but in Southeast Asia
breakfast is an extension of everyday
street food culture Savory fresh and
deeply rooted in Tradition a typical
morning might be rice porridge topped
with minced pork or a steaming bowl of
noodle soup made with pork or beef broth
fresh herbs and lime in Cambodia
breakfast is fast fresh and eaten at a
bustling Street stall so to start our
trip here we decided to do the
same all right so this morning we found
qu and I'm sorry sorry if I'm saying
that horribly basically noodle soup it's
pretty popular breakfast around here so
I've heard literally just stopped in the
first place we could see that looked
like it had good food and it actually is
pretty good this is very good it's a
huge portion and it was only 6,000 real
we're just splitting one between the two
of us there's noodles and green onions
and we got chicken sprouts and a really
yummy bra and we go a freet tea hot it's
like we finished slurping down our
noodles and ventured into the heart of
the market in search for a chare style
coffee it's similar to Vietnamese coffee
in that it's served with sweet condensed
milk it's a rich creamy and slightly
bitter
taste it's like almost
chocolatey doesn't taste like
coffee it's almost like the a really
specific type of like chocolate soft
serve ice cream I had as a child so what
we're finding out here is the language
barrier is a little bit more challenging
to navigate in the local markets is
expected but we
got iced coffee I think this is the
traditional style and they make it like
a Cambodian style coffee
but it tastes like
[Music]
chocolate now for any Western Travelers
Cambodia is going to feel incredibly
inexpensive case and point our next stop
set us back exactly $1 in a world where
a buck barely covers a bottle of water
here it buys you an
experience right now we're at WNAM which
is built on the only Hill in the city
and it's actually an artificial Hill
that was built in
1373 I think Legend has it that in 1372
a very wealthy woman asked all the
inhabitants to build up this hill and
then about 100 years later in the 1400s
the city of pom pen was built around it
the air inside the temple is thick with
a scent of burning incense intricate
gold statues sit beneath a ceiling
painted with colorful murals locals come
here to pray light candles and leave
offerings hoping for good fortune but
just outside we had an unexpected
encounter our very first Horn Bill and
it wasn't
[Music]
alone it is a Temple site so I recommend
covering your shoulders and knees I
haven't seen anything officially saying
that and I've seen people walking around
in shorts and with their shoulders
covered but just out of respect we're
always going to encourage more coverage
at religious sites I did however see
someone get asked to take their hat off
in the temple itself so keep that in
mind but this is a very cool spot it has
a lot of shade which is nice but there's
these big freaking
birds so we just wrapped up at the
temple and now
we're look at that wao
[Music]
oh getting around panan pin is pretty
intense I mean just listen to
it at least from what we've seen there
aren't a lot of traffic lights or stop
signs so every intersection is basically
like a free-for-all and then if you want
to cross the street
you're basically playing Frogger with a
hope and a prayer what do you think is
worse the driving in Bangkok or panen
panom pen for sure yeah Bangkok
Lake there's a tiny bit more
order here it's like there's suggestions
for traffic and then if you follow them
you follow them if not you don't and to
be fair to Cambodia the streets don't
seem nearly as crowded as they're
neighbors the men here are very taken
with Tony I feel like multiple times a
day there's a different man coming up
and call telling him how handsome he is
which I agree but it's really funny this
also happened in Malaysia
[Music]
[Applause]
too we officially made it to the Royal
Palace and if you're driving along the
river of panon pen you really can't miss
it it is actually closed from 12:00 to
2:00 so we grab some lunch we have like
20 more minutes until it's back open but
this is a complex of buildings that
actually serves as the official
residence of the king of Cambodia we
walked a few blocks to find some food
something quick maybe some fried noodles
and some curry soup but even with the
stop for food we still had time to chill
so let's talk about a little scam that
goes on here and to be fair this isn't
some isolated thing in Cambodia you'll
find it all over southeast Asia and it
starts with the Tuk Tok drivers so just
like with Thailand be aware of Tuk tuck
drivers they may even be a little bit
more aggressive than in Thailand but
they'll hang out in places like this
where a lot of tourists go luckily for
us we knew that the Royal Palace would
be closed from 12:00 to 2: but like one
of the drivers came up to him was was
like hey it's closed till 2:30 which you
know it's part of this game they try to
get you say it's closed and take you
somewhere else that's way further away
so you pay extra money but that's not
the case we know it's coping that too
last night I learned a couple of phrases
like Tay and teon which I think means no
thank you please I know I'm saying that
horribly though and even with the
phrases it doesn't really help they'll
still kind of follow you around some of
them are actually kind of nice and
they'll strike up a conversation but
with others it feels just a little bit
too aggressive it's not the most
Sinister scam in the world but it's
definitely annoying if you are not
expecting it that said I get it people
got families to feed and hustling
tourist is just part of the game but we
have no problem giving a little extra to
a driver that won't try to hustle us
anyway the palace is open now and we
have to go get tickets
did you imagine having this many people
just walk through your yard tickets here
were 10 bucks a person USD like Tony
said they're closed from 1200 to 2 we
entered right at 2 and there was still
hordes of people so it's a huge property
it doesn't feel like crowded but it's
definitely not empty you do need to be
dressed appropriately so shoulders and
knees covered so keep that in mind as
well
this is potentially a dumb question but
I'm going to ask it anyway with all
these complexes which one does the king
actually live in or does he actually
live here at all I'm sure I can Google
that later on but if you know the answer
to that just leave a comment down
below so the Royal Palace is one of a
few different places in Cambodia you can
go to to get like an overview of the
history here now the national museum is
right around the corner from here but we
decided to come to the Royal Palace
because they both cost $10 a person so
it was a little bit out of our budget to
try to do
[Music]
both being midday we didn't last long in
this heat and after a quick and
unsuccessful search for the royal family
we made a stop at a little museum by the
exit thoughts on the Royal
Palace this is interesting I think the
price was a little steep but
I think I'd prefer like a to go visit
temples versus The Palace if you've got
extra
cash it's interesting we had a few hours
before Sunset but our plan was set we're
heading to the famous or maybe even
Infamous basak Lane but until then we
did the only logical thing kicked back
and waited for the sunset
[Music]
we were hungry and looking for food so
we made our way to boson Lane now we got
here by ordering a tuck tuck through the
grab app it only cost us 5,800 reel
which is like a125 which is crazy but
this area is supposed to be famous for
the NTI so we have two missions find
good food and maybe find some
beers theck Lane is a neon lit alley of
bars and speak easys that truly come
alive at night and as a surprise to No
One the first thing on our mind is food
so we stopped into Mama
wangs we kick things off with the pork
and cabbage dumplings and yes they were
amazing the texture was perfectly chewy
with just the right amount of meat
stuffed inside they're like my Trader
Joe's dumplings but better then I got
stir-fried noodles and Tony ordered
kungpow chicken I don't know what to say
it's just good like a little bit crispy
on the outside tender chicken a rock
with it the one downside to Bak Lane is
is you'll find more tourist prices here
especially at the restaurants still
under 20 bucks for a great meal it's
hard to
complain after dinner we decided to bar
hop and put a little Theory to the test
they say you can find beer here for a
dollar and we're about to find
out Cambodia
Cambodia the same that's good $1
here that's pretty good first Beer's
down now for a long journey to our next
[Music]
location we're here we grabbed another
Cambodian beer but this time we switched
it up and tried something a little
different a German beer brewed right
here in Cambodia because why
[Music]
not it's almost like a
shandy tasting mhm
it feels like it tastes like it should
be more than a
[Music]
dollar and for our final stop we had to
try anchor beer cambodia's most famous
brew and the best part is that it only
set us back $150 so cheers to that six
beers total three each for about 8 bucks
the price of one in the states not bad
Cambodia we capped our night with three
beers and hopped on a Tuk Tuk back to
our place
we got more to see in the morning plus
we don't need anyone in the comments
saying we need an intervention over a
couple of
beers
[Music]
okay at any rate tomorrow things get a
bit more
serious I know it's a bit jarring to go
from a high to a low point but we
believe learning the history of any
place you visit should be a requirement
for any traveler the fun the food the
night life it's all part of the
experience but so is understanding what
a place has been through so today we're
visiting toast Lang to educate ourselves
on cambodia's dark past a past that is
still deeply felt
today so we're at tolang also not as S21
this was the genocide Center here where
basically the chimer Rogue regime
imprisoned somewhere between 10,000 and
20,000 people between 1975 and
1979 is when this atrocity happened
happened and just being here is a little
bit of a reality check as you can see we
opted for the audio tour it is $10 a
person so $20 for the both of us if you
don't want the audio tour it's $5 a
person it would have been $10 for the
both of us some of the spaces inside
they ask you not to record or take
pictures so we're going to honor that
but even from the outside in the few
areas where photography is allowed
visiting S21 is one of the most sobering
and haunting experiences you can have in
PanAm
pen what was once a high school became a
torture prison under the kimar Rouge
where thousands of innocent people were
imprisoned interrogated and executed
walking through the empty cells you see
the Rusted bed frames the shackles and
the black and white photographs of the
victims it's impossible not to feel the
weight of what happened here that took
almost exactly 2 hours of our afternoon
we I remember pressing play at 1:00 and
we exited a little before 3 um I would
say that this is a must visit in fren
not only to you understand just the very
recent history which is devastating but
to also pay your respects to what
happened here I think it's definitely
worth the money we visited S21 before
The Killing Fields which I think is a
good order this gave us a lot of context
for when we go to visit The Killing
Fields later this
weekend it's definitely possible to see
tol slang and The Killing Fields in the
same day but for us that was just too
heavy so for now let's fast forward to
when we came back out this time for
something a little lighter pens Night
Market just across the street from the
river and probably a block or so from
the Old Market is the panom pen night
market now this is supposed to be a
really good place for cheap food so this
is where we're going to try to have
dinner tonight we got here right when it
opened so it's a little sleepy right now
hopefully it picks up a little later
like the other markets you can also buy
clothes and other items here or if you
want to just hang out for a couple hours
they have a little match you can sit on
I think you might have to order food
first but it's probably pretty cheap
things started to pick up as the sun
began to set so we grabb one of those
mats and started trying to figure out
our next meal been practicing for this
[Music]
moment see how long I last like
[Music]
this it's a lot more comfortable than it
used to be though so I think I'm okay
Sarah on the other hand makes it look
easy so Sarah got Patti with chicken I
got the fried noodles with chicken each
one of these I think was about
10,000 R plus two mango shakes which was
hot the night market is packed with food
stalls each offering a slightly
different version of the same thing but
the way I see it you really can't go
wrong just pick one and roll the dice in
the end I guess it all comes down to How
It's Made and who's making it anyway we
browse through some clothes at the
market and walk back to our
hotel tomorrow we visit The Killing
Fields and it's a heavy day ahead and
it's one we want to be mentally prepared
for
okay uh the L
person include audio audio guys thank
you where you from
us let want to get audio okay thank you
for the next part of this video viewer
discretion is advised so we made our way
to The Killing Fields we wanted to do
S21 in The Killing Fields on different
days because obviously the topic is a
little bit heavy and The Killing Fields
is kind of difficult to get to it took
us about an hour to get here from from
where we were staying in PanAm pen but
like we were saying yesterday if you go
to the genocide Museum you should also
come here this is not an easy place to
visit but if you do want to visit for
foreigners it costs $6 a person and that
also includes an audio tour that you get
with it the first stop on the grounds is
a pagonia housing the skeletal remains
of the victims who were Unearthed from
the fields we won't be filming much here
either out of respect but I wanted to
show this part because hearing about
atrocities like this is one thing but
actually seeing the sheer number of
victims puts it into an entirely
different perspective
Ive so every time I try to think about
just how recently all this happened it's
kind of hard to wrap my mind around it
like this was just over 40 years ago me
personally I didn't learn this in high
school I don't know if you learned
anything about this in high school like
I didn't even know about about this and
then now I'm looking up past events and
trying to figure out just what exactly
happened here and it's just crazy to me
that stuff like this isn't taught like
not even just and I don't want to like
bottle just this one instance but not
even just this genocide but a lot of
genocides that happen in the world
aren't taught in our schools especially
in the US so I think this is an
important part of history that everyone
should know panon pen wasn't always easy
it was eye openening challenging and at
times heavy but that's what travel is
supposed to be not just a highlight reel
but a way to push yourself and to
understand a place beyond what's on the
surface we came here for the food the
energy and the adventure but we're
leaving with something more a deeper
appreciation for cambodia's resiliency
its people and its history and the best
part we've barely even scratched the
surface there is so much more of
Cambodia to explore from the ancient
temples of anarat to quiet Countryside
Villages and hidden beaches so this
isn't good because it's just the
beginning we'll see you in the next one
